ai-without-jargon.instruction: Write a prompt asking an AI to explain a complex business concept in plain language — no jargon, no acronyms.
ai-without-jargon.hint: Explain machine learning to a small business owner who has never used AI. Use an analogy from running a retail store. No technical terms. Under 150 words.
ai-without-jargon.goodExample: Explain machine learning to a small business owner with no tech background. Use a retail store analogy. No acronyms, no technical terms. Keep it under 150 words and end with one real example they could use tomorrow.
ai-without-jargon.successMessage: Jargon-free communication is a business superpower. If the AI can explain it simply, you can explain it to your team.

ai-without-jargon.instruction: Rewrite this jargon-heavy sentence into plain English: 'We need to leverage AI to optimize our omnichannel customer journey through predictive analytics and NLP-driven sentiment analysis.'
ai-without-jargon.placeholder: Say the same thing in plain English...
ai-without-jargon.hint: We want to use AI to understand what our customers need across our website, stores, and app — by predicting what they'll want next and reading the mood of their feedback.
ai-without-jargon.badExample: We need to leverage AI to optimize our omnichannel customer journey through predictive analytics and NLP-driven sentiment analysis.
ai-without-jargon.goodExample: We want to use AI to give customers a better experience everywhere they interact with us — website, store, app — by predicting what they need and understanding how they feel from their feedback.
ai-without-jargon.successMessage: If your team can't understand the plan, they can't execute it. Plain language isn't dumbing down — it's leadership.

what-ai-can-and-cannot-do.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to evaluate whether a specific business task is a good fit for AI automation. Be specific about the task.
what-ai-can-and-cannot-do.hint: Evaluate whether AI can automate our customer support email triage. We get 200 emails/day across 5 categories: billing, technical, returns, feedback, and spam. Currently 2 people spend 3 hours/day sorting them. What can AI handle vs. what still needs a human?
what-ai-can-and-cannot-do.goodExample: Evaluate whether AI can automate our email triage. 200 emails/day, 5 categories (billing, technical, returns, feedback, spam). 2 staff spend 3 hours/day sorting. What can AI reliably handle? What needs a human? What's the risk of errors for each category?
what-ai-can-and-cannot-do.successMessage: Knowing what AI CAN'T do is just as valuable as knowing what it can. This kind of honest evaluation prevents costly mistakes.

what-ai-can-and-cannot-do.instruction: List 3 tasks in your work that AI could help with AND 2 tasks where a human is essential. Explain why for each.
what-ai-can-and-cannot-do.hint: AI can help: 1) Drafting first versions of reports (saves time, human reviews). 2) Sorting customer feedback by topic (pattern recognition). 3) Generating social media captions (creative starting points). Humans needed: 1) Final hiring decisions (judgment, legal liability). 2) Handling angry VIP clients (empathy, relationship context).
what-ai-can-and-cannot-do.badExample: AI can help with stuff. Humans are needed for other stuff.
what-ai-can-and-cannot-do.goodExample: AI can help: 1) Drafting first versions of weekly reports — it's pattern recognition on structured data, and a human reviews before sending. 2) Sorting 200+ customer feedback emails by topic — AI catches themes I'd miss. 3) Generating social media caption options — gives me 5 starting points instead of staring at a blank page. Humans essential: 1) Final hiring decisions — legal liability, gut instinct about culture fit, reading between the lines of an interview. 2) Handling escalated client complaints — empathy, relationship history, and judgment calls that AI can't make.
what-ai-can-and-cannot-do.successMessage: This is strategic AI thinking — knowing where to deploy it and where to protect the human touch.

find-your-ai-opportunities.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to audit your daily workflow and identify automation opportunities. Describe your typical day.
find-your-ai-opportunities.placeholder: Here's my typical workday... find the AI opportunities...
find-your-ai-opportunities.hint: Here's my typical day as a small business owner: 8am check emails (45 min), 9am update inventory spreadsheet (30 min), 10am write social media posts (1 hr), 11am respond to customer questions (1 hr), 1pm update bookkeeping (45 min), 2pm create quotes for clients (1 hr). Identify which tasks AI could help with, estimate time saved, and rank by easiest to implement.
find-your-ai-opportunities.badExample: How can AI help my business?
find-your-ai-opportunities.goodExample: My daily workflow: emails (45 min), inventory updates (30 min), social media writing (1 hr), customer Q&A (1 hr), bookkeeping (45 min), client quotes (1 hr). Which tasks can AI assist? Estimate time saved per task. Rank by ease of implementation: quick win to complex.
find-your-ai-opportunities.successMessage: You just created an AI opportunity audit. The tasks with the most time spent and the most repetition are your biggest wins.

find-your-ai-opportunities.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to suggest the single highest-impact AI use case for a specific type of business.
find-your-ai-opportunities.placeholder: What's the #1 AI use case for a...
find-your-ai-opportunities.hint: I own a 15-person accounting firm. We spend most of our time on tax prep, client communication, and document review. What is the single highest-impact AI use case we should implement first? Consider: time saved, ease of adoption, client satisfaction impact, and cost.
find-your-ai-opportunities.goodExample: I own a 15-person accounting firm. Main time sinks: tax prep, client communication, document review. What's the single highest-impact AI use case to implement first? Evaluate by: time saved, adoption difficulty, client satisfaction impact, and cost.
find-your-ai-opportunities.successMessage: Starting with ONE high-impact use case beats trying to AI-ify everything at once. Focus wins.

your-first-week-with-ai.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to create a 5-day plan for introducing AI tools into your work. Be specific about your role and current tools.
your-first-week-with-ai.placeholder: Create a 5-day AI adoption plan for...
your-first-week-with-ai.hint: I'm a real estate agent who currently uses Google Workspace and Canva. Create a 5-day plan to start using AI in my work. Day-by-day breakdown. Each day: one specific task to try with AI, the tool to use, and expected time saved. Keep it simple — I'm not technical.
your-first-week-with-ai.badExample: How do I start using AI?
your-first-week-with-ai.goodExample: I'm a real estate agent using Google Workspace and Canva. Create a 5-day AI adoption plan. Each day: one task to try with AI, which tool to use, 15-minute time commitment max. I'm not technical — keep it practical. Format: Day 1-5 with task, tool, and expected outcome.
your-first-week-with-ai.successMessage: A structured plan removes the overwhelm. Day by day, you'll build AI into your muscle memory.

your-first-week-with-ai.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to help you do something you'd normally spend 30+ minutes on. Time yourself — can AI cut it in half?
your-first-week-with-ai.hint: I need to write a proposal for a potential client. They're a local gym that wants a new website. Usually this takes me 45 minutes. Draft a 1-page proposal including: project scope (5-page website), timeline (4 weeks), 3 deliverables, and a professional sign-off. Tone: confident and friendly.
your-first-week-with-ai.goodExample: Draft a 1-page website proposal for a local gym client. Scope: 5-page site (Home, About, Classes, Trainers, Contact). Timeline: 4 weeks. Include 3 key deliverables and pricing placeholder. Tone: confident, friendly, professional. This usually takes me 45 minutes — let's cut that in half.

Draft a 1-page website proposal for a local gym client. Scope: 5-page site (Home, About, Classes, Trainers, Contact). Timeline: 4 weeks. Include 3 key deliverables, a pricing placeholder, and a professional sign-off. Tone: confident, friendly, professional. This usually takes me 45 minutes — I want a solid first draft to edit.
your-first-week-with-ai.successMessage: When AI handles the first draft, you become the editor instead of the writer. That's the time-saving superpower.

ai-tools-landscape.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to recommend the right tools for your specific business needs. Include your budget, team size, and main pain points.
ai-tools-landscape.hint: Recommend AI tools for a 5-person marketing agency. Budget: $200/month total for AI tools. Pain points: writing client reports takes too long, social media content creation is slow, and we waste time on email. Suggest 3 tools max, explain what each solves, and confirm they work together.
ai-tools-landscape.badExample: What AI tools should I use?
ai-tools-landscape.goodExample: Recommend AI tools for a 5-person marketing agency. Budget: $200/mo total. Pain points: slow client reports, slow social content creation, email overload. Max 3 tools. For each: what it solves, monthly cost, learning curve (easy/medium/hard), and whether they integrate with Google Workspace.
ai-tools-landscape.successMessage: Specific constraints (budget, team size, pain points) prevent the classic mistake of buying tools you don't need.

ai-tools-landscape.instruction: You're choosing between ChatGPT Plus ($20/mo) and Claude Pro ($20/mo) for your business. Write a prompt asking AI to help you decide based on YOUR specific use cases.
ai-tools-landscape.placeholder: Compare these tools for my specific needs...
ai-tools-landscape.hint: Compare ChatGPT Plus and Claude Pro for a freelance consultant who mostly needs: long document analysis (10+ page contracts), writing client proposals, and data analysis from CSV files. Which is better for each use case? I can only afford one. Give me a clear recommendation with reasoning.
ai-tools-landscape.goodExample: I'm a freelance consultant choosing between ChatGPT Plus and Claude Pro ($20/mo each, can only pick one). My use cases: 1) Analyzing 10-page contracts 2) Writing client proposals 3) CSV data analysis. Which tool is better for each? Give me a clear winner with specific reasoning.
ai-tools-landscape.successMessage: Tool selection should be based on YOUR use cases, not generic reviews. You just wrote a decision-making prompt.

the-roi-calculator.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to calculate the ROI of implementing an AI tool in your workflow. Include real numbers.
the-roi-calculator.placeholder: Calculate the ROI of using AI for...
the-roi-calculator.hint: Calculate the ROI of using Claude Pro ($20/month) for my freelance copywriting business. Currently I spend: 10 hours/week writing first drafts, 5 hours/week on client emails, 3 hours/week on proposals. My hourly rate is $75. If AI cuts each task by 40%, what's my monthly ROI? Show the math.
the-roi-calculator.goodExample: Calculate ROI for Claude Pro ($20/mo) in my copywriting business. Current time: 10 hrs/wk writing drafts, 5 hrs/wk emails, 3 hrs/wk proposals. My rate: $75/hr. If AI cuts each by 40%, what's my monthly time saved, dollar value saved, and ROI percentage? Show the math.
the-roi-calculator.successMessage: ROI with real numbers beats gut feelings. Now you can justify the investment to yourself — or your boss.

the-roi-calculator.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to identify HIDDEN costs of NOT using AI in your business — the cost of inaction.
the-roi-calculator.placeholder: What's the cost of NOT using AI for...
the-roi-calculator.hint: I run a 10-person customer support team. We manually answer 500 tickets/day, average response time is 4 hours, and we lose about 5% of customers who say our responses were too slow. Calculate the hidden costs of NOT using AI: customer churn revenue loss, overtime costs, and opportunity cost of staff doing repetitive work instead of high-value tasks.
the-roi-calculator.goodExample: I run a 10-person support team. 500 tickets/day, 4-hour avg response time, 5% customer churn citing slow responses. Calculate hidden costs of NOT using AI: revenue lost to churn, overtime costs, and opportunity cost of staff doing repetitive triage instead of complex problem-solving.
the-roi-calculator.successMessage: The cost of inaction is often bigger than the cost of the tool. This framing helps make the business case.

measuring-ai-success.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to create KPIs for measuring the success of an AI implementation in a specific department.
measuring-ai-success.placeholder: Create KPIs for AI success in...
measuring-ai-success.hint: Create 5 KPIs to measure the success of AI-assisted content creation in our marketing department. For each KPI: what to measure, how to measure it, target improvement percentage, and timeline. Include both efficiency metrics (time saved) and quality metrics (engagement, conversion).
measuring-ai-success.badExample: How do I know if AI is working?
measuring-ai-success.goodExample: Create 5 KPIs for measuring AI-assisted content creation success in marketing. For each: what to measure, measurement method, target improvement %, and 90-day timeline. Mix efficiency metrics (time/cost) and quality metrics (engagement/conversion). Format as a table.
measuring-ai-success.successMessage: What gets measured gets managed. These KPIs turn 'AI is helping' from a feeling into a fact.

measuring-ai-success.instruction: Describe one specific way you could measure whether AI saved you time this week. Be concrete — what's the before and after?
measuring-ai-success.hint: Before AI: Writing my weekly client report took 90 minutes every Friday. I'd gather data from 3 sources, write summaries, and format it. After AI: I paste the raw data into Claude and ask for a formatted report. Total time: 25 minutes. Time saved: 65 minutes/week = 4.3 hours/month.
measuring-ai-success.goodExample: Before AI: Writing my weekly client report took 90 minutes every Friday. I'd gather data from 3 sources, write summaries, and format everything manually. After AI: I paste the raw data into Claude and ask for a formatted summary with key insights highlighted. I review and adjust the tone. Total time: 25 minutes. Time saved: 65 minutes/week, or 4.3 hours/month — that's half a workday I get back.
measuring-ai-success.successMessage: That's a measurable win. Track these weekly and you'll build an airtight case for AI investment.

build-vs-buy.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to evaluate whether you should build a custom AI solution or buy an existing tool for a specific business need.
build-vs-buy.placeholder: Should I build or buy an AI solution for...
build-vs-buy.hint: I need an AI chatbot for my e-commerce store that answers product questions and helps with returns. Should I build a custom one using the OpenAI API or buy a ready-made solution like Intercom or Tidio? My constraints: $500/month budget, 1 developer on staff, need it live in 30 days, 200 customer conversations/day.
build-vs-buy.goodExample: Evaluate build vs. buy for an AI chatbot on my e-commerce store. Needs: answer product questions, handle returns. Constraints: $500/mo budget, 1 developer, live in 30 days, 200 conversations/day. Compare: custom build (OpenAI API) vs. Intercom vs. Tidio. Factor in: cost, time to launch, maintenance burden, and scalability.
build-vs-buy.successMessage: Build vs. buy is one of the most expensive decisions a business makes. This structured evaluation prevents regret.

build-vs-buy.instruction: Think of an AI need in your work. List 3 reasons to BUILD custom and 3 reasons to BUY off-the-shelf. Which wins?
build-vs-buy.hint: Need: AI email sorting. Build reasons: 1) Perfect fit for our categories 2) No monthly subscription 3) We own the data. Buy reasons: 1) Live in 1 day vs 3 months 2) Already tested by thousands 3) Includes support and updates. Winner: BUY — speed to value matters more than perfect customization at our stage.
build-vs-buy.badExample: Build is better because it's custom.
build-vs-buy.goodExample: Need: AI email sorting for our support inbox. Build reasons: 1) We can train it on our exact 12 ticket categories for perfect accuracy 2) No recurring subscription — one-time development cost 3) We own the model and data, no vendor lock-in. Buy reasons: 1) Live in 1 day vs. 3 months of development 2) Already battle-tested by thousands of companies 3) Includes ongoing support, updates, and new features we'd have to build ourselves. Winner: BUY — at our stage (15-person team, no ML engineer), speed to value and maintenance burden matter more than perfect customization.
build-vs-buy.successMessage: You just did a build-vs-buy analysis. The answer usually depends on your stage — startups buy, scaled companies build.

ai-policy-template.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to draft a simple AI usage policy for a small team. Include what data is OK and not OK to share with AI tools.
ai-policy-template.placeholder: Draft an AI usage policy for...
ai-policy-template.hint: Draft a simple AI usage policy for a 20-person marketing agency. Cover: what data employees CAN share with AI tools (public content, general questions, anonymized data), what they CANNOT share (client NDAs, financial data, employee records, passwords), and guidelines for reviewing AI output before sending to clients. Keep it to 1 page. Tone: helpful, not scary.
ai-policy-template.goodExample: Draft a 1-page AI usage policy for a 20-person agency. Sections: 1) Approved uses (drafting, brainstorming, research). 2) Prohibited data (client NDAs, financials, PII, passwords). 3) Output review requirements before client delivery. 4) Approved tools list. Tone: helpful, not fear-based. Format: bullet points, plain English.
ai-policy-template.successMessage: A clear AI policy protects your business AND empowers your team to use AI confidently. This is leadership.

ai-policy-template.instruction: Write a prompt asking AI to create a quick-reference card that employees can keep at their desk — 'AI Do's and Don'ts.'
ai-policy-template.hint: Create a quick-reference 'AI Do's and Don'ts' card for our team. 5 Do's (like: use AI for first drafts, always fact-check output) and 5 Don'ts (like: never paste client contracts, never use AI output without review). Keep each point to one sentence. Add a 'When in Doubt' section with who to ask.
ai-policy-template.badExample: What are the rules for AI?
ai-policy-template.goodExample: Create a desk reference card: 'AI Do's and Don'ts.' 5 Do's, 5 Don'ts, each one sentence. Include a 'When in Doubt' section with escalation contact. Format for printing on a single index card. Plain English, no jargon.
ai-policy-template.successMessage: A reference card on every desk means the policy actually gets followed. Simple beats comprehensive.
